1: goral livnei Yehudah lemishpechotam (goral, H1486; mishpachah, H4940): 'the lot for the tribe of Judah by their clans.' Goral (lot, casting lot) is the sacred means of territorial allocation — the lot was cast before YHWH so that the distribution of the land was understood as divine determination, not human negotiation (Num 26:55-56; Josh 14:2). Mishpachot (clans/families) is the social unit between tribe (shevet) and household (bayit) — the extended kinship group that received territory collectively. Judah's territory is defined first because Judah was the preeminent tribe (Gen 49:8-12; Num 2:3), and Caleb — the faithful spy — belonged to Judah (Josh 14:6).
2: yam hamelach (yam, H3220; melach, H4417): 'the Salt Sea.' The Dead Sea is consistently called yam hamelach (Sea of Salt) in the OT — named for its extreme salinity, which makes it uninhabitable by ordinary marine life. It defines the southern-eastern boundary of Judah's territory. The Dead Sea sits at approximately 430 meters below sea level, the lowest point on earth's surface. In prophetic imagination it becomes the sea into which YHWH's judgment flows (Ezek 47:8-11): the salt sea that receives living water from the temple becomes fresh and teeming with fish — the reversal of the death-characteristic that gives it its name.
3: ma'aleh aqrabbim (ma'aleh, H4608; aqrab, H6137): 'the ascent of Akrabbim (Scorpion Pass).' Ma'aleh (ascent, going up) identifies a climbing pass through the Negeb highlands south of the Dead Sea. Aqrabbim (scorpions) names the pass for its characteristic inhabitants — the rocky Negeb terrain is still scorpion habitat. The boundary description in vv.2-12 follows a geographical survey of the land in four directions: south (vv.2-4), east (v.5a), north (vv.5b-11), west (v.12). The careful boundary demarcation reflects ANE land-grant practice: official documents specified boundaries precisely to prevent future dispute.
4: vehayah tochalot hagvul hayam haGadol: 'the boundary ends at the Great Sea.' The yam haGadol (Great Sea) is the Mediterranean — 'great' by contrast to the smaller inland bodies of water. The southern boundary of Judah runs from the southern tip of the Salt Sea westward through the Negeb wilderness to the Mediterranean coast. The phrase zeh yihyeh lachem gevul negev (this shall be your southern boundary) provides the formula closing a boundary section. The geographical precision of the boundary descriptions reflects the real administrative needs of the tribal allocations: disputed territory needed documented borders.
5: gevul-qedmah yam hamelach: 'the eastern border is the Salt Sea to the end of the Jordan.' The eastern boundary is the simplest — the Salt Sea (Dead Sea) itself forms a natural barrier. ufnot tzafon: 'turning northward' — the formula signals a pivot from the eastern to the northern boundary. The Jordan River's mouth into the Dead Sea is the northeast corner of Judah's territory. The natural boundaries (sea, river, desert) of Judah's territory explain its historical stability as a political unit — natural defenses that the hill country and the Shephelah reinforced.
6: Beit haAravah (aravah, H6160): 'Beth-arabah' (House of the Arabah). Aravah is the Hebrew name for the Jordan valley depression — the deep rift running from the Sea of Galilee to the Gulf of Aqaba. The Arabah is arid, below sea level, and geologically dramatic — the visible reminder of the rift created by YHWH's action in history (the Sodom and Gomorrah region was in the southern Arabah). The stone of Bohan son of Reuben (v.6) is a landmark placed by a Reubenite and used as a Judah/Benjamin boundary marker — the kind of territorial landmark typical of ancient surveying practice.
7: Devir (Devir, H1688): Debir appears here as a boundary landmark in the Achor Valley. Devir likely means 'back room' or 'oracle room' — related to davar (word/speak) by some interpreters, suggesting a connection with prophetic or priestly speech. The Valley of Achor (Achor = 'trouble') is named for the Achan incident (ch7: the troubling of Israel). That the boundary passes through the Valley of Trouble is significant: the site of Israel's first great covenant failure becomes a boundary marker within Judah's inheritance. Hosea will later transform Achor into a door of hope (Hos 2:15).
8: ge Ben-Hinnom (gei, H1516; Hinnom, H2011): 'the Valley of the Son of Hinnom.' This valley on Jerusalem's southwestern flank becomes one of the most significant geographical features in Israel's later history. Under Manasseh and Ahaz it was the site of child sacrifice to Molech (Tofet, 2 Kgs 23:10; Jer 7:31). Josiah defiled it to prevent its further use (2 Kgs 23:10). In NT period, Ge Hinnom became Geenna (Gehenna) — the word Jesus used for the place of final judgment. The valley that began as a boundary marker between Judah and Benjamin became the topographical basis for the eschatological concept of divine judgment.
9: Qiryat Ye'arim (qiryah, H7151; ya'ar, H3293): 'City of the Forests.' Kirjath-jearim (Kiriath-jearim) is a significant site: the ark of the covenant rested here for twenty years after its return from the Philistines (1 Sam 7:1-2) before David brought it to Jerusalem (2 Sam 6:2-4). The 'city of forests' on the Judah-Benjamin border became the storage site of YHWH's presence-symbol during one of Israel's darkest spiritual periods — YHWH's ark located at a boundary city, neither in the central shrine nor in captivity, waiting for the reign of the king after YHWH's own heart.
10: har Seir (seir, H8165): 'Mount Seir' — not Edom's mountain but a ridge near Kirjath-jearim in Judah. The name Seir (hairy/rough, related to se'ar/hair) appears for multiple locations in the OT. The boundary continues westward through the hill country toward the coastal plain, passing through Beth-shemesh (House of the Sun — where the Philistines returned the ark, 1 Sam 6:12-15). The boundary towns along this north-western edge of Judah will be the frontier zone of Philistine-Israelite conflict throughout the period of the judges and early monarchy.
11: veyatza gevul el-keter har haefer: 'the border goes out toward the shoulder of Mount Ekron.' The northern boundary of Judah ends at the Mediterranean coast, including the Philistine cities. Yatza (go out, extend) is the standard direction verb for boundary descriptions — the boundary 'goes out' from landmark to landmark. The Philistine coast (Ekron, Ashdod, Gaza — vv.45-47) will remain contested territory throughout the period of the monarchy. The formal allocation of the coastal plain to Judah did not translate into effective control; the Philistines maintained their hold despite the legal grant.
12: yam haGadol ugevul: 'the Great Sea and its coast.' The western boundary is simply the Mediterranean coastline — YHWH's natural boundary marker. The four-direction survey of Judah's territory (south, east, north, west) gives the territory a shape: from the Negeb wilderness in the south, the Dead Sea in the east, the northern hill country, and the Mediterranean coast. The ideal extent of Judah's territory encompasses the entire southern half of Canaan; actual effective control varied enormously across the periods of judges, united monarchy, divided monarchy, and exile.
13: ish haAnakim (anaq, H6061): 'the man of the Anakim.' Caleb's special inheritance — a portion within Judah's allotment — is granted because of his faithfulness at Kadesh (Num 14:24; Josh 14:9-12). Ish haAnakim refers to the Anakim (the giant population), and Arba was their father — Hebron was literally 'the city of Arba, the father of Anak.' Caleb requested and received the most fortified, most fearsome part of the territory — the mountain city defended by the giants that the faithless spies had cited as evidence of impossibility. The man who said 'we can certainly do it' (Num 13:30) goes first to the hardest task and succeeds.
14: vayoresh misham Kalev et-shloshut bnei ha'Anaq (yarash, H3423): 'Caleb drove out from there the three sons of Anak.' Yarash (dispossess, drive out, take possession) is the conquest's governing verb — the word that describes what YHWH commanded Israel to do throughout Canaan. The three sons of Anak (Sheshai, Ahiman, and Talmai) were the same figures that the twelve spies had reported forty-five years earlier (Num 13:22). Caleb, at eighty-five years old (Josh 14:10), did what the entire first generation had been afraid to do: he drove them out. The length of the list (three named sons) emphasizes the thoroughness of the dispossession.
15: Devir (Devir, H1688): 'Debir.' Debir (also called Kirjath-sefer in this verse) is the second city Caleb attacks — moving south from Hebron into the Shephelah. Kiriyat-sefer (City of the Book/Scroll, H7153) is the older name for Debir. The name may indicate a scribal center or archive — a city associated with written records. That the city of scribes/records was one of the first to be taken in Caleb's territory is suggestive: the conquest begins with the city of writing. Debir will later become a Levitical city (Josh 21:15), its archives serving the covenant community's priestly learning.
16: natan lo et-Achsah bito le'ishah (natan, H5414): 'he gave him Achsah his daughter as wife.' Caleb's offer — his daughter as prize for the conqueror of Kirjath-sefer — is the ANE convention of prize-marriage for military achievement. The offer is not degrading by the standards of the time but honors both the daughter (she will be given to a man of demonstrated valor) and the enterprise (the conquest is urgent enough to merit the highest incentive). Achsah (ankle-bracelet, from akhes/H5895) is one of the named women of the conquest narrative whose action proves decisive.
17: Otniel ben-Qenaz achi Kalev: 'Othniel son of Kenaz, Caleb's brother.' Othniel will become Israel's first judge (Judg 3:9-11), the prototypical judge who delivers Israel from oppression when they cry to YHWH. His introduction here — as the man who conquered the City of Books — establishes his military credentials before the period of the judges begins. Kenaz is the name of both Othniel's father and Caleb's father (Josh 14:6) — their relationship is either brothers or uncle-nephew depending on how achi is read. The overlap between Caleb's clan and the first judge introduces the Kenizzite tradition that underlies early Judahite leadership.
18: vatisiteihu litshol me'et aviyha sadeh (sut, H5496; sha'al, H7592): 'she urged him to ask her father for a field.' Sut (urge, incite) is a strong word — Achsah pressured Othniel to request a field from Caleb. The detail that she then dismounts her donkey herself to make the request directly suggests that Othniel's request was either insufficient or she decided to advocate personally. Vatitzneach me'al hachamor (she dismounted from the donkey) — the act of dismounting before asking was a gesture of respect before a superior. Achsah's negotiating initiative is remembered in the narrative as admirable resourcefulness, not impropriety.
19: gullot mayim (gullah, H1543; mayim, H4325): 'springs of water.' Achsah's request for both upper and lower springs — in addition to the field in the Negeb — is a request for the most valuable resource in the semi-arid south: water. Gullot (springs, from galal/to roll — the rolling up of water from the ground) are natural spring sources, not cisterns. Caleb grants her 'upper springs and lower springs' — the complete water supply for the dry Negeb territory. The narrative's detail about water rights reflects the genuine agricultural concern: land without water in the Negeb is worthless; land with springs is life-giving. Caleb's generosity with the water mirrors YHWH's generosity with the land.
20: zot nachalat matteh bnei Yehudah lemishpechotam (nachalah, H5159; matteh, H4294): 'this is the inheritance of the tribe of Judah by their clans.' Nachalah (inheritance, possession) is the OT's most important land-ownership term — it carries the sense of perpetual, inalienable possession. The land is not purchased or earned but inherited as YHWH's gift to the covenant community. Matteh (tribe, also staff/rod) links the tribe to its symbol of authority. The formula 'by their clans' (lemishpechotam) applies the allocation to the extended kinship groups who will actually settle and work the territory.
21: ve'areihem min-qetzeh matteh bnei Yehudah: 'their cities from the extreme south of the tribe of Judah.' The Negeb district cities (vv.21-32) — approximately twelve cities in the extreme south — represent Judah's southernmost settlements, bordering the wilderness. Many of these cities appear in later narratives: Beersheba (v.28) was the traditional southern boundary of Israel ('from Dan to Beersheba'), Ziklag (v.31) was David's city as a Philistine vassal (1 Sam 27:6), and Hormah (v.30) was the site of Israel's first victory after the wilderness period (Num 21:3). The city list is not merely administrative but encodes the geography of Israel's early history.
22: Qinah veChatzevon ve'Avadah: Kinah, Dimonah, and Adadah. The southern Negeb cities are mostly unidentified today — their locations are debated and few have been conclusively excavated. The list preserves the administrative geography of early Judah, giving modern archaeology reference points for the distribution of early Iron Age settlements in the Negeb. The preservation of these place names — many of them obscure — reflects the faithfulness of the biblical tradition in recording what was administratively true at the time of composition, even when the names have since been lost.
23: veQedesh veChatzor veYitnan: Kedesh, Hazor, Ithnan. Qedesh (holy place) appears as both a personal name and a place name throughout the OT — the name indicates a site with cultic significance, designated as set-apart. Multiple places named Kedesh appear in the tribal territories, reflecting the widespread practice of designating cultic sites with this generic name. The repetition of Hazor (which appears in multiple districts) reflects either a naming tradition for multiple settlements or textual conflation over the centuries of transmission.
24: Zif veTelem ve'Bealot: Ziph, Telem, Bealoth. Ziph (v.24, 55) appears twice in Judah's city list — once in the Negeb district and once in the hill country district. The Ziph of the hill country (v.55) is the one associated with David's fugitive period (1 Sam 23:14-24; 26:1-3) — the Ziphites twice betrayed David's hiding place to Saul. The administrative geography of Judah's city list thus provides the backdrop for the David narratives: the specific districts and cities named in Joshua 15 appear repeatedly in Samuel, confirming the Sitz im Leben of the tribal records.
25: Chatzor Chadatah veQeriyot Chetzron hi Chatzor: 'New Hazor and Kerioth-hezron (that is, Hazor).' The parenthetical identification ('that is, Hazor') distinguishes this settlement from other Hazors. The naming practice of 'new' towns (chadatah, new/fresh) alongside older settlements reflects the growth and reorganization of settlements over time. Keriyot (towns) may be related to the Kerioth that appears in later prophetic judgment oracles (Jer 48:24,41 for Moab; Amos 2:2) — the geographic name persisting across contexts as a common noun for settlements.
26: Amam veShema veMotzadah: Amam, Shema, Moladah. Moladah (moledah, birth/lineage) reappears in the Simeonite city list (Josh 19:2) and was reoccupied after the exile (Neh 11:26). The border zone between Judah and Simeon was fluid: Simeon's tribal territory was within Judah's (Josh 19:1 'within the inheritance of Judah'), so many cities appear in both lists. The administrative overlap reflects the demographic and political absorption of Simeon by Judah that characterized the early period — Simeon as a tribe effectively disappears into Judah in the later biblical record.
27: veChatzar Gadah veCheshmon uBeit-Pelet: Hazar-gaddah, Heshmon, Beth-pelet. Chatzar (enclosure, village) is a common element in Negeb place names (Hazar-gaddah, Hazar-shual, Hazar-susah) — the word indicates a walled enclosure or hamlet rather than a full city (ir). The Negeb was populated by such semi-nomadic settlements surrounding water sources. The use of chatzar rather than ir for many of these southern settlements reflects the genuinely pastoral and semi-nomadic character of the southern Negeb landscape.
28: Beeir Sheva uvYizta ve'Yeruet: Beer-sheba, Biziothiah, Baalah. Beeir Sheva (Well of Seven / Well of the Oath) was the most important city of the Negeb — the traditional southern boundary of Israel ('from Dan to Beersheba,' Judg 20:1). The well associated with Abraham's covenant with Abimelech (Gen 21:31-32) and with Jacob's theophany on the way to Egypt (Gen 46:1-4) is the first named city in the Negeb district. Beer-sheba is the Negeb's administrative and cultic center — the place where the patriarchal tradition gave Israel its sense of southern identity.
29: Baalah veIyim ve'Atzem: Baalah, Iim, Ezem. Baalah (lady/mistress, feminine of Baal) appears as both a place name and an alternate name for Kirjath-jearim (Josh 15:9-10). Multiple Balahs in the tribal lists reflect the pre-Israelite cultic geography: sites named for Baal (the Canaanite storm deity) were common throughout the land. The Israelite administrative geography did not immediately rename Baal-sites — many retained their pre-conquest names well into the monarchy period. Prophetic critique of the Baal names came later (Hos 2:17: 'she will call me "my husband" and no longer call me "my Baal"').
30: veEltolad veKesil vecHormah: Eltolad, Chesil, Hormah. Chormah (devoted to destruction, from charam/H2763 — the cherem) is named for the total destruction of its Canaanite population: the word is the verbal noun of herem (the sacred ban). Hormah appears in Numbers 14:45 and 21:3 — twice in connection with decisive military engagements. The conquest name (cherem) preserved in the place name is a permanent reminder of the theological character of the conquest: YHWH's judgment on the Canaanite cities was not mere military conquest but sacred destruction.
31: Tziqlag veMatmannah veSansannah: Ziklag, Madmannah, Sansannah. Tziqlag (Ziklag) becomes historically significant in the David narratives: the Philistine king Achish gave it to David as his base during his Philistine service (1 Sam 27:6), and the text notes 'it has belonged to the kings of Judah to this day' — a reference to the time of composition, when it was still a Judahite royal possession. The detailed city list in ch15 thus provides the administrative framework within which the David narratives operate; the geography was precisely remembered.
32: veLevavot veShilchim ve'Ayin veRimon: Lebaoth, Shilhim, Ain, Rimmon. The formula at the close of the Negeb district: 'all the cities with their villages' — twenty-nine cities total. The round number signals the completion of an administrative district count. Iyr ve'chatzereicha (cities and their villages) is the standard phrase for urban settlement with dependent agricultural hamlets — the city provides defense, the surrounding villages provide agricultural production. The Negeb district's twenty-nine cities represent the administrative reality of early Judahite settlement in the south.
33: baShfelah (Shefelah, H8219): 'in the Shephelah (Lowlands).' The Shephelah is the foothills between the coastal plain and the Judean highlands — a transitional zone of gentle hills, wadis, and fertile valleys. It was the most contested region of Judah's territory throughout the monarchic period: the main valleys (Aijalon, Sorek, Elah, Guvrin) provided access routes between the Philistine coast and Jerusalem. David's fight with Goliath was in the Elah Valley (1 Sam 17:2) — a Shephelah corridor. The distinction between Shephelah (lowland hills) and har (mountain/highland) reflects the precise geographic consciousness of the biblical writers.
34: Eshtaol uTzor'ah veAshnah: Eshtaol, Zorah, Ashnah. Zorah and Eshtaol are the towns associated with Samson (Judg 13:2,25; 16:31) — the strongman judge whose territory was precisely the Shephelah border region between Judah/Dan and the Philistines. The geographical precision of the Samson narratives corresponds to the city list here: the places named in Joshua 15 appear as living settings in Judges 13-16. The city lists of Joshua are not merely administrative records but the geographical substructure of Israel's entire narrative tradition.
35: Yarmut ve'Adullam: Jarmuth and Adullam. Adullam (adulam, possibly 'witness of the people') is significant in the David narratives: the Cave of Adullam was David's first refuge when fleeing Saul (1 Sam 22:1-2), and it gathered his first band of followers — those who were distressed, indebted, and discontented. The cave that became the nucleus of the future king's following lay within Judah's Shephelah territory, specifically in this district. Jarmuth was one of the five Amorite kings who allied against Gibeon (Josh 10:3-5), making this district the zone of that famous battle.
36: Sharaim ve'Aditayim vehaGederah: Shaaraim, Adithaim, Gederah. Sha'arayim (two gates) is mentioned in the Goliath narrative (1 Sam 17:52) — the Philistines fled 'to Shaaraim' after David's victory. The city-list entries in vv.33-36 thus correspond precisely to the Elah Valley area of the first Shephelah district. The sixteen cities of this district (v.36) with their villages represent the northern Shephelah, the most frequently contested land in Judah's territory due to its access routes and agricultural fertility.
37: Tz'nan veChadashah uMigdal-Gad: Zenan, Hadashah, Migdal-gad. The second Shephelah district (vv.37-41) covers the central and southern foothills. Migdal (tower/stronghold, H4024) appears as a prefix in numerous place names: Migdal-gad, Migdol (Exod 14:2), Migdal-el (Josh 19:38) — the tower was the most important defensive feature of an ancient walled town, so its name became part of the town's identity. The Shephelah's defensive towers guarded the valley approaches into Judah's interior.
38: Dilean vehaMizpeh veYoqte'el: Dilean, Mizpeh, Joktheel. Mitzpah (watchtower, lookout point, from tzafah/H6822 — to watch/spy) appears frequently as both a place name and a common noun. Multiple Mizpehs exist in the tribal territories, all named for their elevated, watchful position. Mizpeh served as an assembly point in crisis situations (Judg 20:1; 1 Sam 7:5-6) — the watch-point becoming the gathering-point when Israel needed to come together. The name's function (watching, observing) reflects the strategic position of hills in the pre-modern Levant.
39: Lachish veBozqat ve'Eglon: Lachish, Bozkath, Eglon. Lakhish is one of the most archaeologically significant cities in the Shephelah — excavations at Tell Lachish reveal continuous occupation from the Early Bronze Age. Lachish was the second most important city in Judah after Jerusalem during the monarchy and was heavily fortified. It was the focus of the Assyrian siege under Sennacherib (depicted in the Lachish reliefs, now in the British Museum) and was one of the last Judean cities to fall to Babylon (Jer 34:7). Its appearance in the city list alongside Eglon (the Amorite king Joshua defeated in ch10) grounds the list historically.
40: veKabbon veLachmam veKitlish: Cabbon, Lahmas, Chitlish. The second Shephelah district continues with settlements whose later biblical attestation is sparse — they represent the ordinary agricultural hinterland of the major cities like Lachish. The lack of later narrative attention to these towns does not reduce their historical reality: they were inhabited, enumerated, and administered. The biblical text's willingness to record obscure settlements alongside famous ones reflects the genuine administrative character of the source material behind the city lists.
41: veGederot Beit-Dagon veNa'amah uMaqedah: Gederoth, Beth-dagon, Naamah, Makkedah. Beit-Dagon (House of Dagon) is named for the Philistine grain deity — another pre-Israelite Canaanite name preserved in the Israelite administrative geography. Makkedah (v.41) is the cave site where Joshua executed the five Amorite kings who had hidden from the battle (Josh 10:16-17) — the execution site becomes a named settlement within Judah's territorial records. The sixteen cities of the second Shephelah district close with a formula identical to the first.
42: Livnah veEter veAshan: Libnah, Ether, Ashan. Livnah (white, from lavan/whiteness) was a Levitical city (Josh 21:13) and a place of prophetic significance: the Levitical city on the border of the Shephelah and the coastal plain. Libnah revolted against Judah during Jehoram's reign (2 Kgs 8:22), suggesting a distinct local identity even within the Judahite tribal framework. Ether and Ashan appear also in Simeon's city list (Josh 19:7), again reflecting the fluid boundary between Judah and Simeon in the southern Shephelah.
43: veYiftach veAshna vaNetziv: Iphtah, Ashnah, Nezib. The third Shephelah district (vv.42-44) covers the southern foothills connecting to the Negeb. The orderly progression through districts — northern Shephelah, central Shephelah, southern Shephelah — reflects careful geographical organization in the original administrative records. The repetition of similar names in different districts (two Ashnabs, for example) confirms independent sources behind the city list rather than a single invented compilation — a scribe creating a list would not repeat names so confusingly.
44: veQe'ilah veAchziv veMaresheh: Keilah, Achzib, Mareshah. Qe'ilah (Keilah) is associated with David: he rescued it from the Philistines (1 Sam 23:1-12) only to discover that its inhabitants would hand him over to Saul if given the chance. The city David saved was prepared to betray him — a microcosm of the covenant community's relationship with its shepherd. Mareshah was a major administrative center in the Shephelah and a border fortress — Asa defeated the Cushite army near Mareshah (2 Chr 14:9-10).
45: Eqron uchotzeroteiha uvir'oteiha (chatzerot, H2691; ir'ot/gevurot, H1380): 'Ekron with its towns and villages.' The Philistine pentapolis cities (Ekron, Ashdod, Gaza — vv.45-47) are formally allocated to Judah but were effectively under Philistine control throughout the period of the judges and early monarchy. Chatzerot (towns, hamlets — the agricultural dependent villages) and ir'ot (villages, from the root 'forest/grove') represent the two levels of rural settlement: the fortified hamlet and the open village. The inclusion of the Philistine cities in Judah's formal allocation acknowledges the ideal extent of the land grant even when effective control was absent.
46: meEqron veyammah kol asher al-yad Ashdod vechatzeroteiha: 'from Ekron to the sea, all that lay near Ashdod and its villages.' The Philistine coastal plain between Ekron and Ashdod is allocated to Judah — the coastal strip that includes the most fertile agricultural land in the region (the Shephelah lowlands transition to the coastal plain). Ashdod was one of the Philistine pentapolis cities and the site where the ark of the covenant was taken after the battle of Ebenezer (1 Sam 5:1-7). The detailed allocation of this contested region reflects the legal ideal; the narrative tradition documents the gap between ideal and reality.
47: Ashdod chatzeroteiha viviroteiha Azah chatzeroteiha viviroteiha: 'Ashdod with its towns and villages; Gaza with its towns and villages.' Gaza (Azzah) and its territory through the Wadi of Egypt (nahal Mitzrayim, the traditional southwestern boundary of Canaan) closes the Shephelah allocation. Gaza was the southernmost and most important of the Philistine pentapolis — the city where Samson's story ends (Judg 16:21) and where the Philistine temple of Dagon was pulled down. The allocation of Gaza to Judah was ideal geography; effective control of Gaza eluded Israel throughout the biblical period.
48: uvehar (har, H2022): 'in the hill country.' The hill country (Judean highlands) district opens the third and most important geographical zone of Judah's territory. Har (mountain, hill, hill country) designates the elevated limestone plateau running north-south through Judah — the natural fortress that made Judah the most defensible territory in Canaan. The hill country cities (vv.48-60) represent the heartland of Judah, including Hebron (v.54), the patriarchal center. The Judean highlands became the nucleus of the Davidic monarchy and the site of Jerusalem itself.
49: Dannah veQiryat-Sanah hi Devir: 'Dannah and Kiriath-sannah (that is, Debir).' The identification of Kiriath-sannah with Debir connects it to Caleb's conquest (vv.15-17) — the city has multiple names: Kiriath-sefer (City of the Book), Kiriath-sannah, and Debir. The multiple names may reflect different naming traditions from different periods, preserved in the administrative record. Sanah (possibly 'thorn' or 'branch') as an alternative name suggests a different local tradition about the city's character than the 'book' association of Kiriath-sefer.
50: ve'Anav veEshtemo veAnim: Anab, Eshtemoh, Anim. Anab (v.50) is notable in the context of the Anak: Anav likely means 'grapes' but its proximity to Anak (the giant people) may not be coincidental. Joshua had killed the remaining Anakim in the hill country, including at Anab (Josh 11:21). Eshtemoh (Eshtemoa) became a Levitical city (Josh 21:14) and appears in the David narratives: David sent gifts to Eshtemoa from the Ziklag spoil (1 Sam 30:28). The Levitical presence in the hill country district reflects the deliberate distribution of Levites throughout Judah's heartland.
51: veGoshen veChalon veGiloh: Goshen, Holon, Giloh. Goshen here is a Judahite city (not the Egyptian region), probably named by settlers who remembered the name from the Egyptian experience. The naming of a Canaanite city after a place associated with Israelite slavery is an act of memory: the land of abundance that Egypt had been (Goshen was the fertile delta region) is now re-placed in the inheritance of Judah. Ahithophel, David's wise counselor who betrayed him and later hanged himself (2 Sam 15:12; 17:23), was from Giloh.
52: Arav uDumah veEshan: Arab, Dumah, Eshan. Dumah (silence/stillness) as a place name reflects a location with a distinctive quality — perhaps the silence of a hidden valley or the stillness of a water source. The same name appears in the prophetic oracles against foreign nations (Isa 21:11 'the oracle concerning Dumah') and as a son of Ishmael (Gen 25:14), reflecting the multiple uses of the name across different traditions. The Judean hill country's city names reflect a mixture of geographical description, cultic heritage, and personal commemoration.
53: veYanum uveit-Tappuach ve'Afekah: Janum, Beth-tappuah, Aphekah. Beit-Tappuach (House of the Apple) reflects the horticultural character of the Judean highlands: the mountain climate and limestone soil supported apple and other fruit orchards. The naming of towns after their agricultural products (House of the Apple, House of the Vineyard, etc.) is common in the Judean hill country city list — a practical recording of what made each site agriculturally valuable. Tappuach (apple/quince) appears also as a border town of Ephraim (Josh 16:8; 17:8).
54: Chumtah veQiryat Arba hi Chevron veTs'ior: 'Humtah, and Kiriath-arba (that is, Hebron), and Zior.' Kiryat Arba (City of Four or City of Arba) is identified with Hebron — the city named for its 'four' components (possibly four quarters or four associated settlements) or for Arba, father of the Anakim (v.13). Hebron (Chevron, H2275 — from chaver/partner, association, fellowship) was the patriarchal center: Abraham purchased the cave of Machpelah there (Gen 23), and it was the first capital of David's kingdom (2 Sam 2:1-4). Its appearance in the hill country list as both Kiriath-arba and Hebron represents the dual naming tradition.
55: Maon veKarmel veZif veYuttah: Maon, Carmel, Ziph, Juttah. Ma'on (dwelling place) and Karmel (garden/orchard of God) are hill country towns associated with Nabal (1 Sam 25:2-3): 'there was a man in Maon whose business was in Carmel.' The David narratives are again embedded in the geography of Joshua 15 — the specific towns of the fifth hill country district provide the setting for the Abigail story. Ziph in this district (v.55) is the hill country Ziph of the David-Saul chase narratives (1 Sam 23:14-24), distinct from the Negeb Ziph of v.24.
56: veYizre'el veYoqde'am veZanoach: Jezreel, Jokdeam, Zanoah. The Jezreel here is a hill country city in Judah, distinct from the famous Jezreel Valley in the north (the site of Ahab's vineyard, Elijah's confrontation with Baal, and Jehu's coup). The existence of a southern Jezreel (Yizreel, God sows) within Judah's hill country, while the northern Jezreel dominated the great valley, reflects the common practice of reusing meaningful place names across different tribal territories.
57: haQa'in Giv'ah veTimnah: Kain, Gibeah, Timnah. Giv'ah (hill) appears repeatedly as a place name throughout the Judean and Benjaminite highlands — the hill is the natural feature that defines settlement sites in this terrain. Multiple Gibeahs exist: Gibeah of Judah (here), Gibeah of Benjamin (site of the Benjaminite war, Judg 19-20), Gibeah of Saul (1 Sam 10:26). The proliferation of Gibeah-names reflects the pervasive hill-country terrain of the central highlands; the specific Gibeah is usually identified by its tribal affiliation.
58: Chalhul Beit-Tzur uGedor: Halhul, Beth-zur, Gedor. Beit-Tzur (House of the Rock) was a major administrative center and fortress in the Judean hill country — excavations reveal occupation from the Middle Bronze Age and significant fortification during the Maccabean period (the Apocrypha records battles there). The fortress function of Beth-zur in later periods reflects its strategic importance at the junction of the north-south mountain ridge road and the lateral roads toward Hebron. The city list records a geographic reality that archaeological evidence confirms.
59: uMa'arat veBeit-Anot veEltekon: Maarath, Beth-anoth, Eltekon. The sixth hill country district closes with six cities. Some LXX manuscripts include an additional district not in the MT between vv.59 and 60, containing cities in the Jerusalem area (including Tekoa, Ephrathah/Bethlehem, and Kiriath-jearim) — a textual tradition suggesting that an entire Bethlehem district may have been accidentally omitted from the MT. The possible omission of a Bethlehem district from the MT is particularly striking given Bethlehem's later theological significance as David's birthplace and the anticipated birthplace of the Messiah (Mic 5:2).
60: Kiryat-Baal hi Kiryat-Ye'arim uhaRavah: 'Kiriath-baal (that is, Kiriath-jearim) and Rabbah.' Kiriath-jearim's alternate name — Kiriath-baal (City of Baal) — reveals its pre-Israelite cultic character: a city dedicated to the Canaanite deity. The identification of the Baal-city with the City of Forests (Kiriath-jearim) demonstrates how place-name traditions overlap: the same city carries both its Canaanite religious name and its geographical name. That the ark of the covenant rested at a city whose alternate name was 'City of Baal' (1 Sam 7:1-2) is a detail the text records without comment — YHWH's presence was not deterred by a Baal place-name.
61: uvamidbar Beit haAravah Middin veSechacah: 'in the wilderness, Beth-arabah, Middin, and Secacah.' The wilderness district (vv.61-62) identifies settlements in the Judean desert east of the central highland ridge — the barren zone between the mountains and the Dead Sea. Beit ha'Aravah (House of the Desert Depression) and Secacah and others may correspond to sites near the Dead Sea, including possibly the Qumran area where the Dead Sea Scrolls community later established itself. The Judean wilderness was never uninhabited: its caves, cliffs, and seasonal water sources supported herding communities and, in later periods, religious communities seeking separation from the world.
62: veNivshan veir haMelach ve'Ein Gedi: 'Nibshan, the City of Salt, and En-gedi.' Ein Gedi (Spring of the Kid) is the most significant site in the wilderness district — a dramatic spring and oasis on the western shore of the Dead Sea, where fresh water cascading down the cliff face creates a tropical microclimate in the desert. En-gedi is where David hid from Saul in the caves (1 Sam 23:29; 24:1-7) — the wilderness oasis as the place of the fugitive king's refuge. The Song of Songs begins with 'I am a cluster of henna blossoms from the vineyards of En-gedi' (Song 1:14) — the oasis's fertility inspiring poetic comparisons.
63: vehaYevusi yeshvei Yerushalayim lo-yachlu bnei Yehudah lehorisham (yavas, H2983; Yerushalayim, H3389; yarash, H3423): 'the Jebusites, who inhabited Jerusalem, the children of Judah could not drive out.' Lo-yachlu lehorisham (could not dispossess them) is the chapter's most theologically significant phrase: despite the formal allocation of Jerusalem to Judah, and despite earlier military action (Judg 1:8 records burning the city), the Jebusites were not dispossessed. Jerusalem remained Jebusite until David captured it (2 Sam 5:6-7). The Jebusite stronghold at the heart of the land allocation is the clearest example of the gap between the ideal grant and the actual possession — a gap that required the Davidic king to finally close.
